did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) such as EfficientNet, ResNet, and DenseNet, combined with fully connected layers for metadata processing, to perform skin lesion classification (diagnosis of skin cancer) using dermoscopy images and patient metadata.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation and comparison of hierarchical, flat, and mixed neural network models for skin cancer diagnosis using the ISIC 2019 dataset. It details specific methodologies (CNNs, metadata fusion), experimental setups, and reports new quantitative results (BACC scores) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Diagnosis of Skin Cancer Using Hierarchical Neural Networks and Metadata
brief_summary: This study investigates the diagnosis of skin cancer by integrating patient metadata with dermoscopy images using hierarchical, flat, and mixed neural network models. Utilizing the ISIC 2019 dataset, the authors evaluated various Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) combined with metadata fusion techniques to classify eight skin lesion types. The experimental approach compared the performance of these models using Balanced Accuracy (BACC) as the primary metric. Results indicated that including metadata significantly improved diagnostic performance, with the best mixed model achieving a BACC of 80.76%. Although flat models generally outperformed hierarchical ones overall, the hierarchical approach proved beneficial for specific lesion classes and narrowing down subtypes. The study concludes that combining hierarchical structures with metadata offers a promising direction for improving automatic skin cancer diagnosis systems.

lesion_types_studied.details: Intermediary classification level in the hierarchical model, distinguishing from non-melanocytic lesions.
Intermediary classification level in the hierarchical model, distinguishing from melanocytic lesions.
Intermediary classification level in the hierarchical model, distinguishing from malignant lesions.
Intermediary classification level in the hierarchical model, distinguishing from benign lesions.
Final diagnosis class in the ISIC 2019 dataset; melanocytic benign lesion.
Final diagnosis class in the ISIC 2019 dataset; melanocytic malignant lesion.
Final diagnosis class in the ISIC 2019 dataset; benign non-melanocytic lesion.
Final diagnosis class in the ISIC 2019 dataset; malignant non-melanocytic lesion.

Final diagnosis class in the ISIC 2019 dataset; benign non-melanocytic lesion.
Final diagnosis class in the ISIC 2019 dataset; benign non-melanocytic lesion.

all_task_types.target: Eight discrete skin lesion classes (Nevus, Melanoma, Dermatofibroma, Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Benign keratosis, Vascular) and intermediate hierarchical categories (melanocytic vs non-melanocytic, benign vs malignant).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using dermoscopy images and patient metadata. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories using softmax outputs for class probabilities. The task involves flat classification into 8 classes, hierarchical classification with intermediary binary/multiclass decisions, and mixed models that select between flat and hierarchical class predictions based on confidence thresholds.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: The database from the ISIC 2019 challenge used to train and evaluate the proposed models. It contains dermoscopic images and patient metadata (age, gender, location).

use.training: The original training dataset was divided into a training set (80%) and a validation set (20%) for model training and hyperparameter selection.
use.validation: Used to choose hyperparameter values and evaluate CNN architectures during the experimental setup.
use.testing: The held-out test set provided by the challenge organizers was used to test the best models obtained after training.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and compare the performance of different models (flat, hierarchical, and mixed) by giving equal importance to all classes, calculated as the average of Sensibility (SE) for each class.
To measure the ratio of positive samples correctly classified as positive for each class, used to calculate Balanced Accuracy.
Used in Mixed Model 2 to determine if hierarchical probabilities should be preferred over flat model predictions based on a percentage threshold T.
Used in Mixed Model 3 to assess decision confidence by calculating the difference between the two highest softmax probabilities, compared against a parameter P.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using Balanced Accuracy (BACC) scores without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019 Validation Set.metric: Balanced Accuracy (BACC) and Sensibility (SE) per class
ISIC 2019 Validation Set.notes: Mixed Model 1 achieved the highest BACC. Flat models generally outperformed hierarchical models. The inclusion of metadata improved performance across all fusion methods compared to images only. Mixed Model 1 transferred 59.12% of cases to the flat model, which had a BACC of 84.58% on those transferred lesions.

ISIC 2019 Test Set.metric: Sensibility (SE) per class and Balanced Accuracy (BACC)
ISIC 2019 Test Set.notes: Models suffered a decrease of roughly 25% in BACC scores compared to the validation set. NV and BCC were the lesions with the best performance. Mixed Model 1 with the modified hierarchy performed slightly better than the original hierarchy in the test set. The majority of lesions did better with the modified hierarchy in the mixed 1 model on the test set.

architecture_used.main_model: Hierarchical, Flat, and Mixed Neural Network models utilizing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for image processing and Fully Connected Layers (FCLs) for metadata processing.
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; standard CNN architectures (ResNet, DenseNet, EfficientNet) were evaluated.

fusion_methods.name: Concatenation
fusion_methods.description: Features from images and metadata are concatenated into a single vector.

fusion_methods.name: Multiplication
fusion_methods.description: Metadata goes through an FCL with 'd' neurons (dimension of last CNN layer), then multiplied element-wise with image features.

fusion_methods.name: Image Feature Reducer
fusion_methods.description: Reduces image features based on ratio 'r' and metadata features 'm'. Image output goes through FCL with 'n' neurons (ReLU), metadata through FCL with 'm' neurons, then concatenated.

hierarchical_structure.classifier_e: Ternary classifier (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)

mixed_models.name: Mixed Model 1
mixed_models.description: Direct competition between flat and hierarchical models; selects decision based on highest softmax probability.

mixed_models.name: Mixed Model 2
mixed_models.description: Compares hierarchical probabilities against a threshold T.

mixed_models.name: Mixed Model 3
mixed_models.description: Compares difference between two highest softmax probabilities against parameter P.

classification_or_output_head.flat_model: Single softmax layer with 8 neurons (one for each class).
classification_or_output_head.hierarchical_model: Multiple softmax layers: 2 neurons for binary classifiers (a, b, c) and 3 neurons for ternary classifiers (d, e).

conclusions: The inclusion of patient metadata (age, gender, location) has a significant positive impact on the diagnostic performance of both flat and hierarchical neural network models for skin cancer diagnosis.
Flat models generally outperformed hierarchical models in terms of overall Balanced Accuracy (BACC) on the validation set (79.08% vs 73.53%).
Hierarchical models provided a rationale for decisions and improved performance for specific lesion classes, particularly melanocytic lesions (Nevus and Melanoma), compared to flat models.

The proposed Mixed Model 1, which allows flat and hierarchical models to compete directly based on confidence, achieved the highest validation BACC of 80.76%, demonstrating that combining these approaches improves overall system performance.

Reducing the original three-level hierarchical structure to a two-level hierarchy (modified hierarchy) proved beneficial for certain lesions and performed slightly better than the original hierarchy on the held-out test set.
Metadata alone was insufficient for accurate diagnosis, achieving a low BACC of 27.83%, indicating that dermoscopy images are essential for effective classification.
Among the tested CNN architectures, ResNet networks generally outperformed others when metadata was included, while EfficientNet-B2 was superior when using only images.

The study acknowledges that while hierarchical models offer interpretability and benefit specific subtypes, flat models remain more robust for overall classification, suggesting future work should explore hybrid structures or different hierarchical taxonomies.
important_remarks: Significant performance drop on the held-out test set: Balanced Accuracy (BACC) decreased by approximately 25% compared to the validation set (e.g., Mixed Model 1 dropped from 80.76% to 56.41%), indicating potential overfitting or dataset shift.

Lack of ground truth for the test set: The ISIC 2019 test set provided by the challenge organizers did not include labels, preventing direct calculation of test set metrics by the authors; results were obtained via the online platform.

No statistical significance testing: The paper compares models using Balanced Accuracy (BACC) and Sensibility (SE) without reporting p-values or confidence intervals for the differences in performance.
Limited external validation: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2019 dataset; no external validation on independent cohorts was performed to assess generalizability.

Metadata insufficiency: Models using only patient metadata achieved a very low BACC of 27.83%, confirming that dermoscopy images are essential for diagnosis, though metadata provides a positive incremental benefit.

Hierarchical structure limitations: The original three-level hierarchy was found to be suboptimal; a modified two-level hierarchy (eliminating classifiers for benign/malignant non-melanocytic distinction) performed slightly better on the test set, suggesting the clinical taxonomy may not align perfectly with deep learning feature separability.

Class imbalance impact: Certain classes like Vascular (VASC) and Dermatofibroma (DF) had very few samples in the training set (202 and 191 respectively), which may contribute to poor performance on these classes in the test set.
Mixed Model redundancy: Mixed Models 2 and 3 yielded nearly identical results to each other and were deemed redundant compared to Mixed Model 1, which allowed direct competition based on confidence.

Deployment concern regarding false positives: The authors note that while the model may incorrectly diagnose malignant lesions as benign, it rarely diagnoses benign lesions as malignant, which might be clinically less dangerous but still requires careful interpretation.
